subject: Physics
q: A charged particle carrying charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>T</mi></math> exists in the region where the particle is moving then the force on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is :
question: A charged particle carrying charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>T</mi></math> exists in the region where the particle is moving then the force on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is :
solution:
Using the equation of force as,

F→=qV→×B→

F→=10-6×10-3i^j^k^23453-6

=-30i^+32j^-9k^×10-9 N

subject: Physics
q: The fractional change in the magnetic field intensity at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from centre on the axis of current carrying coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> to the magnetic field intensity at the centre of the same coil is: (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>)
question: The fractional change in the magnetic field intensity at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from centre on the axis of current carrying coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> to the magnetic field intensity at the centre of the same coil is: (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>)
solution:
As we know,

Baxis=μ0iR22R2+x23/2  where, R=a  

Bcentre =μ0i2a

∴Baxis =μ0ia22a2+r23/2

∴ fractional change in magnetic field =μ0i2a-μ0ia22a2+r23/2μ0i2a=1-11+r2a23/2

≈1−1−32r2a2=32r2a2

subject: Physics
q: A charged particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $q$ travels on a circular path of radius $r$ that is perpendicular to a magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$. The time taken by the particle to complete one revolution is
question: A charged particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $q$ travels on a circular path of radius $r$ that is perpendicular to a magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$. The time taken by the particle to complete one revolution is
solution:
$\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{Bq} \omega \mathrm{r}$
$\omega=\mathrm{Bq} / \mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{qB}}$

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are equal if: (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are number of turn and current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> respectively)
question: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are equal if: (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are number of turn and current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> respectively)
solution:
As magnetic moment of both the coil is the same,

μ1=μ2

⇒NAIAπrA2=NBIBπrB22⇒NAIA0.102=NBIB0.202⇒IANA=4IBNB

subject: Physics
q:
A wire X of length 50 cm carrying a current of 2 A is placed parallel to a long wire Y of length 5 m. The wire Y carries a current of 3 A. The distance between two wires is 5 cm and currents flow in the same direction. The force acting on the wire Y is :

​​​​​​​
question:
A wire X of length 50 cm carrying a current of 2 A is placed parallel to a long wire Y of length 5 m. The wire Y carries a current of 3 A. The distance between two wires is 5 cm and currents flow in the same direction. The force acting on the wire Y is :

​​​​​​​
solution:
An attractive force will act normal to  X towards Y because the direction of the currents in the wires is the same.

Force exerted on length l due to the magnetic field is given as F=FXY=FYX=I1l1 B12

⇒F=μ0I1I22πrl1

Given here, I1=2 A, I2= 3 A, l1=5 m, r=5 cm.

Putting the values, we have

F=4π×10-7×2×32π×5×10-2×50×10-2

=1.2×10-5 N directed towards wire X.

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an alpha particle of the same velocity enter in a uniform magnetic field which is acting perpendicular to their direction of motion. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by the alpha particle and proton is
question: A proton and an alpha particle of the same velocity enter in a uniform magnetic field which is acting perpendicular to their direction of motion. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by the alpha particle and proton is
solution:
Radius of circular path of charged particle q of mass m moving with velocity v in magnetic field B is given by r=mvqB

rαrp=4mv2eBmveB=2:1

subject: Physics
q:
An electron, moving along the x- axis with an initial energy of 100 eV, enters a region of magnetic field B→=1.5×10-3 Tk^ at S (see figure). The field extends between x=0 and x=2 cm. The electron is detected at the point Q on a screen placed 8 cm away from the point S. The distance d between P and Q (on the screen) is:
(electron’s charge 1.6×10-19 C , mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg )
question:
An electron, moving along the x- axis with an initial energy of 100 eV, enters a region of magnetic field B→=1.5×10-3 Tk^ at S (see figure). The field extends between x=0 and x=2 cm. The electron is detected at the point Q on a screen placed 8 cm away from the point S. The distance d between P and Q (on the screen) is:
(electron’s charge 1.6×10-19 C , mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg )
solution:
R=mVqB
=2m(K.E)qB
mV=2m K.E
=2×9.1×10-31×100×1.6×10-191.6×10-19×1.5×10-3
=2.248 cm≈ 2.25 cm

sin⁡θ=22.248=0.89
PA=R1-cos⁡θ=1.22 cm
AB=6tan⁡θ=11.71 cm
d=PA+AB=12.93 cm

subject: Physics
q: The horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at a place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. A very long straight conductor carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the direction from South east to North West is placed. The force per unit length experienced by the conductor is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at a place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. A very long straight conductor carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the direction from South east to North West is placed. The force per unit length experienced by the conductor is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Horizontal component of magnetic field, BH=3.5×10-5 T

Force acting on wire, F=ilBsinθ, where i=2 A

Therefore, Fl=iBsinθ=2×3.5×10-5×12

=35×10-6 N m-1

subject: Physics
q: A very long solenoid of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is carrying current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> as a function of time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Counterclockwise current is taken to be positive. A circular conducting coil of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is placed in the equitorial plane of the solenoid and concentric with the solenoid. The current induced in the outer coil is correctly depicted, as a function of time, by:
question: A very long solenoid of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is carrying current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> as a function of time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Counterclockwise current is taken to be positive. A circular conducting coil of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is placed in the equitorial plane of the solenoid and concentric with the solenoid. The current induced in the outer coil is correctly depicted, as a function of time, by:
solution:
ϕouter=μ0nKte-αt4πR2
ε=-dϕdt=-μ0nK4πR2(e-αt1-αt)
iinduced=-ce-αt1-αtResistance
At t=0, Iinduced=-ve
From the above equation and initial value of current we can say the correct graph is B.

subject: Physics
q: A straight wire carrying a current of 14 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2.2 cm as shown in the figure. The magnetic field produced by the current at the centre O of the arc is ____ × 10–4 T
question: A straight wire carrying a current of 14 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2.2 cm as shown in the figure. The magnetic field produced by the current at the centre O of the arc is ____ × 10–4 T
solution:
It is given that 

i=14 AR=2.2×10-2 m

The magnetic field at the centre O is

B=μ0i4R=4π×10-7×144×2.2×10-2=2×10-4 T

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section with radius ' $a$ ' carries a steady current $I$. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The plot of magnitude of magnetic field B with distance $r$ from the centre of the wire is given by
question: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section with radius ' $a$ ' carries a steady current $I$. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The plot of magnitude of magnetic field B with distance $r$ from the centre of the wire is given by
solution: We know inside the wire $\begin{array}{ll} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi R^2} \cdot r & (O < r < R) \\ \text { And } B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} & \text { for }(R < r) \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop of wire, supporting a mass $m$, hangs with one end in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ pointing out of the plane of the paper. A clockwise current is set up such that $i>m g / B a$, where $a$ is the width of the loop. Then :
question: A rectangular loop of wire, supporting a mass $m$, hangs with one end in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ pointing out of the plane of the paper. A clockwise current is set up such that $i>m g / B a$, where $a$ is the width of the loop. Then :
options: The weight rises due to a vertical force caused by the magnetic field and work is done on the system.
The weight do not rise due to vertical for caused by the magnetic field and work is done on the system.
The weight rises due to a vertical force caused by the magnetic field but no work is done on the system.
The weight rises due to a vertical force caused by the magnetic field and work is extracted from the magnetic field.
solution: No Solution Available

subject: Physics
q:
A current of 5A exists in a square loop of side $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$. Then the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the square loop will be $p \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$. where, value of p is _____ .
$\left[\right.$ Take $\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{TmA}^{-1}\right]$.
question:
A current of 5A exists in a square loop of side $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$. Then the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the square loop will be $p \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$. where, value of p is _____ .
$\left[\right.$ Take $\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{TmA}^{-1}\right]$.
solution: Let B be the magnetic field due to single side $\begin{aligned} & \text { then } B=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{~d}}\left(\sin \theta_1+\sin \theta_2\right) \\ & =\frac{10^{-7} \times 5 \times 2}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \therefore B_{\text {net }} \text { at centre } O=4 B \\ & =8 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two concentric circular loops of radii r1=30 cm and r2=50 cm are placed in X-Y plane as shown in the figure. A current I=7 A is flowing through them in the direction as shown in figure. The net magnetic moment of this system of two circular loops is approximately
question: Two concentric circular loops of radii r1=30 cm and r2=50 cm are placed in X-Y plane as shown in the figure. A current I=7 A is flowing through them in the direction as shown in figure. The net magnetic moment of this system of two circular loops is approximately
solution:
Net magnetic moment of the system is 

M→=-IA1k^+IA2k^       (Here currents in both circular loops are in opposite direction)

M→=-Iπ0.52k^+Iπ0.32k^   

M→=-7×22725100-9100k^=-2216100k^

 M→=-3.52k^ A m2

=-72k^ A m2

question: A current I ampere flows along an infinitely long straight thin-walled tube, then the magnetic induction at any point inside the tube is
solution: Considering Ampere's loop (shown by dotted line), no current is enclosed by this loop. Therefore, the magnetic field will be zero inside the tube.

subject: Physics
q: A proton with a kinetic energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> moves into a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The angle between the direction of magnetic field and velocity of proton is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>. The pitch of the helical path taken by the proton is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>. (Take, mass of proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and charge on proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>).
question: A proton with a kinetic energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> moves into a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The angle between the direction of magnetic field and velocity of proton is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>. The pitch of the helical path taken by the proton is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>. (Take, mass of proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and charge on proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>).
solution:
The given data is

K.E=2 eV

B=π2×10-3 T

θ=60o

The pitch of the proton is given by

P=2πmqB×vcosθ   ...(i)

By using

 K=mv22⇒v=2Km

Substituting the values in equation (i)

=2π×2mKE×12×21.6×10-19×π×10-3

=2×2×1.6×10-27×2×1.6×10-19×1031.6×10-19

=2×2×10–1=0.4 m

subject: Physics
q: A small point mass carrying some positive charge on it, is released from the edge of a table. There is a uniform electric field in this region in the horizontal direction. Which of the following options then correctly describe the trajectory of the mass ? (Curves are drawn schematically and are not to scale)
question: A small point mass carrying some positive charge on it, is released from the edge of a table. There is a uniform electric field in this region in the horizontal direction. Which of the following options then correctly describe the trajectory of the mass ? (Curves are drawn schematically and are not to scale)
solution: Net acceleration of particle is constant, initial velocity is zero therefore path is straight line.

subject: Physics
q: A small circular loop of conducting wire has radius a and carries current <math><mi>I</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is placed in a uniform magnetic field <math><mi>B</mi></math> perpendicular to its plane such that when rotated slightly about its diameter and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion of time period <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The mass of the loop is <math><mi>m</mi></math> then:
question: A small circular loop of conducting wire has radius a and carries current <math><mi>I</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is placed in a uniform magnetic field <math><mi>B</mi></math> perpendicular to its plane such that when rotated slightly about its diameter and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion of time period <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The mass of the loop is <math><mi>m</mi></math> then:
solution:
τ=MBsin⁡θ=Iα
πR2iBθ=mR22α
ω=2πiBm=2πT;T=2πmiB

subject: Physics
q: Wires 1 and 2 carrying currents $i_1$ and $i_2$ respectively are inclined at an angle $\theta$ to each other. What is the force on a small element dl of wire 2 at a distance of $\mathrm{r}$ from wire 1 (as shown in the figure) due to the magnetic field of wire 1 ?
question: Wires 1 and 2 carrying currents $i_1$ and $i_2$ respectively are inclined at an angle $\theta$ to each other. What is the force on a small element dl of wire 2 at a distance of $\mathrm{r}$ from wire 1 (as shown in the figure) due to the magnetic field of wire 1 ?

subject: Physics
q: A straight wire AB of mass 40 g and length 50 cm is suspended by a pair of flexible leads in uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.40 T as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting leads is _____ A. (Take g=10 m s-2.
question: A straight wire AB of mass 40 g and length 50 cm is suspended by a pair of flexible leads in uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.40 T as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting leads is _____ A. (Take g=10 m s-2.
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic force on the conductor is given by

FB= ILB   ...1

The formula to calculate the gravitational force on the conductor is given by

FG= mg   ...2

Equate both the equations and simplify to obtain the required current through the conductor.

ILB=mg⇒I= mgLB   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required current through the conductor.

I=401000 kg×10 m s-20.5 m×0.40 T=2 A

subject: Physics
q: An electron projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field B moves in a circle. If Bohr's quantization is applicable, then the radius of the electronic orbit in the first excited state is :
question: An electron projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field B moves in a circle. If Bohr's quantization is applicable, then the radius of the electronic orbit in the first excited state is :
solution:
$m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$ ...(i)
$r=\frac{v m}{B q}$ ....(ii)
$\begin{aligned} & n=2 \\ & m r\left(\frac{r B q}{m}\right)=\frac{2 h}{2 \pi} \\ & r=\sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi B q}} \\ & q=e \\ & r=\sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi B e}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
An electron with kinetic energy $5 \mathrm{eV}$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field of 3 $\mu \mathrm{T}$ perpendicular to its direction. An electric field $\mathrm{E}$ is applied perpendicular to the direction of velocity and magnetic field. The value of $E$, so that electron moves along the same path, is _______ $\mathrm{NC}^{-1}$.
$\left(\right.$ Given, mass of electron $=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, electric charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question:
An electron with kinetic energy $5 \mathrm{eV}$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field of 3 $\mu \mathrm{T}$ perpendicular to its direction. An electric field $\mathrm{E}$ is applied perpendicular to the direction of velocity and magnetic field. The value of $E$, so that electron moves along the same path, is _______ $\mathrm{NC}^{-1}$.
$\left(\right.$ Given, mass of electron $=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, electric charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
solution:
For the given condition of moving undeflected, net force should be zero.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{qE} & =\mathrm{qVB} \\ \mathrm{E} & =\mathrm{VB} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{2 \times \mathrm{KE}}{\mathrm{m}}} \times \mathrm{B} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{9 \times 10^{-31}}} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \\ & =4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two thin, long parallel wires separated by a distance 'd' carry a current of 'i' $A$ in the same direction. They will
question: Two thin, long parallel wires separated by a distance 'd' carry a current of 'i' $A$ in the same direction. They will
options: attract each other with a force of $\mu_0 i^2 /(2 \pi \mathrm{d})$
repel each other with a force of $\mu_0 i^2 /(2 \pi \mathrm{d})$
attract each other with a force of $\mu_0 \mathrm{i}^2\left(2 \pi \mathrm{d}^2\right)$
repel each other with a force of $\mu_0 \mathrm{i}^2\left/(2 \pi \mathrm{d}^2\right)$
solution: Using the definition of force per unit length due to two long parallel wires carrying currents.

subject: Physics
q: The electric current in a circular coil of four turns produces a magnetic induction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of single turn, the magnetic induction at the centre of the coil by the same current will be :
question: The electric current in a circular coil of four turns produces a magnetic induction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of single turn, the magnetic induction at the centre of the coil by the same current will be :
solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of the coil is given by, B=μ0i2R.

Now in the first case:

B=4μ0i2R

and for the second case:

B'=μ0i2R'.

As the length of the wire will remain same, therefore

2πR'=42πR⇒R'=4R

Hence, required ratio will be

B'B=R4R'=116

⇒B'=2 T

subject: Physics
q: An electron, a proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are moving in circular orbits of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively in a uniform magnetic field B. The relation between <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: An electron, a proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are moving in circular orbits of radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively in a uniform magnetic field B. The relation between <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution: <math><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi><mi>K</mi></msqrt></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfrac></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>∝</mo><mfrac><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt><mi>q</mi></mfrac></math>. For electron, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mfrac><msqrt><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></msqrt><mi>e</mi></mfrac></math> Where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is the mass of an electron. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo></math>the mass of a proton. For alpha particles, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>α</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mfrac><msqrt><mn>4</mn><mi>m</mi></msqrt><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>e</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mfrac><msqrt><mn mathvariant="italic">m</mn></msqrt><mi>e</mi></mfrac></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi><mo>=</mo></math>charge on proton. For proton, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mfrac><msqrt><mi>m</mi></msqrt><mi>e</mi></mfrac></math>, so, <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math>.

subject: Physics
q: A current carrying circular loop of radius $2$ cm with unit normal $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{k}+\hat{i}}{\sqrt{2}}$ is placed in a magnetic field, $\vec{B} = B_0(3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$. If $B_0 = 4\times 10^{-3}$ T and current $I=100\sqrt{2}$ A, the torque experienced by the loop is ________ Wb·A. ($\pi=3.14$)
question: A current carrying circular loop of radius $2$ cm with unit normal $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{k}+\hat{i}}{\sqrt{2}}$ is placed in a magnetic field, $\vec{B} = B_0(3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$. If $B_0 = 4\times 10^{-3}$ T and current $I=100\sqrt{2}$ A, the torque experienced by the loop is ________ Wb·A. ($\pi=3.14$)
solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of the current carrying loop is given by $\vec{M} = I A \hat{n}$.

Given radius $r = 2$ cm $= 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m, the area of the loop is:
$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (2 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 4\pi \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$

Substituting the given values $I = 100\sqrt{2}$ A and $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$\vec{M} = (100\sqrt{2}) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-4}) \times \left(\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
$\vec{M} = 4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})$ A$\cdot$m$^2$

The torque experienced by the loop in the magnetic field is $\vec{\tau} = \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$.
Given $\vec{B} = 4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$ T, we have:
$\vec{\tau} = [4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})] \times [4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} [(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$

Evaluating the cross product:
$(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k}) = (\hat{i} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{i} \times 2\hat{k}) + (\hat{k} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{k} \times 2\hat{k})$
$= 0 + 2(-\hat{j}) + 3(\hat{j}) + 0 = \hat{j}$

Therefore, the torque is:
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} \hat{j}$

Substituting $\pi = 3.14$:
$\vec{\tau} = 16 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 50.24 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 5024 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ Wb$\cdot$A

subject: Physics
q: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
question: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
solution: Consider ring like element of disc of radius $r$ and thickness $\mathrm{dr}$. If $\sigma$ is charge per unit area, then charge on the element $d q=\sigma(2 \pi r d r)$ current ' $i$ ' associated with rotating charge $\mathrm{dq}$ is $\mathrm{i}=\frac{(\mathrm{dq}) \mathrm{w}}{2 \pi}=\sigma \mathrm{wrdr}$ Magnetic field $\mathrm{dB}$ at center due to element $\mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{dr}}{2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\int \mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega}{2} \int_0^{\mathrm{R}} \mathrm{dr}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{R}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Q} \omega}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \quad\left[\because \mathrm{Q}=\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^2\right] \end{aligned}$ So if $Q$ and $w$ are unchanged then $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{net}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}$ Hence variation of $B_{\text {net }}$ with $R$ should be a rectangular hyperbola as represented in (A).

subject: Physics
q:
An infinitely long hollow conducting cylinder with radius R carries a uniform current along its surface.

Choose the correct representation of magnetic field Bas a function of radial distance r from the axis of cylinder.
question:
An infinitely long hollow conducting cylinder with radius R carries a uniform current along its surface.

Choose the correct representation of magnetic field Bas a function of radial distance r from the axis of cylinder.
solution:
According to Ampere's law,

∮B→·dl→=μ0i

Inside the hollow cylinder, current will be zero. Therefore, magnetic field will also be zero.

Outside the cylinder,

B2πr=μ0i⇒B=μ0i2πr⇒B∝1r

Hence, the correct answer is D.

subject: Physics
q: A wire carrying current I is tied between points P and Q and is in the shape of a circular arc of radius R due to a uniform magnetic field B (perpendicular to the plane of the paper, as shown in the figure) in the vicinity of the wire. If the wire subtends an angle 2θo at the center of the circle (of which it forms an arch) then the tension in the wire is:
question: A wire carrying current I is tied between points P and Q and is in the shape of a circular arc of radius R due to a uniform magnetic field B (perpendicular to the plane of the paper, as shown in the figure) in the vicinity of the wire. If the wire subtends an angle 2θo at the center of the circle (of which it forms an arch) then the tension in the wire is:
solution:
Consider any small element dl of arc of angle dθ . Then magnetic force dF=Idl.B outwards  



For equilibrium of element 

Idl B=2T sindθ2

For small angle sinθ≃θ

IdlB=2Tdθ2

Idl B=TdlR

T=IBR

question: The velocity of certain ions that pass undeflected through crossed electric field $E=7.7 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$ and magnetic field $B=0.14 \mathrm{~T}$ is
solution: As velocity $v=\frac{E}{B}=\frac{7.7 \times 10^3}{0.14}=55 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$

q: A loop of flexible wire of irregular shape carrying current is placed in an external magnetic field. Identify the effect of the field on the wire.
question: A loop of flexible wire of irregular shape carrying current is placed in an external magnetic field. Identify the effect of the field on the wire.
options: Loop assumes circular shape with its plane normal to the field.
Loop assumes circular shape with its plane parallel to the field.
Wire gets stretched to become straight.
Shape of the loop remains unchanged.

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular coil (Dimension <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>×</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) with <math><mn>100</mn></math> turns, carrying a current of <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the clock-wise direction, is kept centered at the origin and in the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> plane. A magnetic field of <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is applied along <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> . If the coil is tilted through <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> about <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , then the torque on the coil is:
question: A rectangular coil (Dimension <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>×</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) with <math><mn>100</mn></math> turns, carrying a current of <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the clock-wise direction, is kept centered at the origin and in the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> plane. A magnetic field of <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is applied along <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> . If the coil is tilted through <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> about <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , then the torque on the coil is:
solution:
Torque= M→×B→
=MBsinθ
=niABsinθ
=100×3×5×10-2×2.5×10-2×1×sin45o
=0.27 N m

subject: Physics
q: Two long, straight wires carry equal currents in opposite directions as shown in figure. The separation between the wires is 5.0 cm. The magnitude of the magnetic field at a point P midway between the wires is _____ μT. (Given: μ0=4π×10-7 T m A-1)
question: Two long, straight wires carry equal currents in opposite directions as shown in figure. The separation between the wires is 5.0 cm. The magnitude of the magnetic field at a point P midway between the wires is _____ μT. (Given: μ0=4π×10-7 T m A-1)
solution:
The direction of the magnetic field at the observation point due to the current carrying conductor is determined by the right-hand cork screw rule.

From the given diagram, it is clear that as the conductors carry current in the mutually opposite direction, the direction of the magnetic fields produced by them at point P will be the same.

Hence, the required magnetic field at P due to the conductors can be calculated as follows:

B=2×μ0i2πa=2×4π×10-7×102π×52×10-2 T=160×10-6 T=160 μT

subject: Physics
q: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is moving with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis under a magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The force acting on the charge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is moving with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis under a magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The force acting on the charge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given:

q=4 μC,v→=4×106 j^ m s-1 and B→=2 k^ T

Force acting on charged particle, F→=q(v→×B→)

=4×10-64×106 j^×2 k^

=4×10-6×8×106i^

⇒F→=32 i^ N

⇒x=32
